steps.title: Trust Is Built in the Routine
steps.body:
When people think about trust, they think about big things: fidelity, honesty about money, showing up during a crisis.

But those are rare tests. Trust is built in the small, boring moments.

• You say you'll take out the trash. You do it.
• You say you'll be home at 6. You're home at 6.
• Your partner asks you to pick up milk. You remember.
• They share something vulnerable. You don't use it against them later.

Every time you follow through on a tiny promise, you deposit a coin in the trust bank.
Every time you don't, you make a withdrawal.

A marriage where both partners reliably do what they say — in small things and big things — is a marriage where trust runs deep.

Trust isn't built in the emergency room.
It's built in the checkout line.

Every promise kept, no matter how small,
is a vote for 'I can count on you.'

questions.label:
Think about the last week. How many small promises did you make — even implied ones?

'I'll call you back.' 'I'll handle that.' 'We should do that soon.' 'I'll be home by 7.'

How many did you follow through on?

Where might your partner feel like they can't count on your follow-through? Be honest.

steps.title: The Trust Equation
steps.body:
Trust = Reliability + Transparency + Benevolence

Reliability — You do what you say you'll do, when you say you'll do it.

Transparency — You share information, even when it's uncomfortable. You don't hide things by omission.

Benevolence — You act in your partner's interest, not just your own. They sense you're on their team.

If reliability is low — the partner feels like they can't count on you.
If transparency is low — the partner feels like they don't really know you.
If benevolence is low — the partner feels like you're looking out for yourself.

Where's your strongest dimension? Where might you need to improve?

steps.question: Mentioning something you did that your partner asked for — but you didn't tell them about — builds trust.
options: False — true reliability doesn't need to be advertised
feedback: It's actually better to just do the thing without announcing it. 'Benevolent hiding' — doing things without needing credit — builds trust more than reporting your good deeds.
Correct. The most trustworthy partners do things without tracking or announcing them. 'I noticed you already took care of X' from your partner is better than 'See, I did X.'

steps.title: Earned vs Demanded Trust
steps.body:
Earned trust: 'You said you'd call after the meeting. You called right after. Over time, I learn I can count on your word.'

Demanded trust: 'Why don't you trust me? I said I would. You should just believe me.'

The difference: earned trust is demonstrated through action over time. Demanded trust asks your partner to ignore their experience and just take your word.

If your partner's trust feels low, don't ask them to trust you more. Ask them what small, consistent actions would demonstrate reliability — then do them.

steps.title: One Trust Deposit Today
steps.prompt:
What's one small thing you can do today to make a trust deposit?

Something your partner has asked for, mentioned wanting, or would be relieved to have handled.

Don't tell them you're doing it. Just do it. The trust grows in the noticing, not the announcing.

Write what you'll do:
steps.body:
What's one small thing you can do today to make a trust deposit?

Something your partner has asked for, mentioned wanting, or would be relieved to have handled.

Don't tell them you're doing it. Just do it. The trust grows in the noticing, not the announcing.

Write what you'll do:

questions.label:
If your partner was asked 'Can you count on them to follow through?' — what would they say?

If the answer is anything less than 'completely,' what's one thing you could change to move that needle?

Not a grand resolution. A specific, small behavior shift.

steps.question: If you have good intentions but struggle with follow-through, your partner should focus on your intentions.
feedback: Correct. Good intentions don't build trust — consistent action does. 'I meant to' doesn't repair broken promises. If you struggle with follow-through, set smaller commitments until your yes is reliable.
Not quite. Intentions matter, but trust is built on outcomes. Your partner experiences your follow-through, not your intentions. Work on making your 'yes' actually mean something.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Connection Week Wrap-Up
steps.body:
You've spent this week rebuilding connection — friendship, vulnerability, play, touch, rituals, and trust.

Tomorrow we wrap it up. You'll reflect on what's changed and prepare for the final week: Mission — designing your shared future.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
